content_of_post_to_comment:
for years the first thing i did every morning, before coffee, before even getting out of bed, was reach for my phone and start scrolling. news app, then instagram, then twitter, then youtube, then a couple newsletters sitting in my inbox. 30 to 45 minutes of thumbing through stuff trying to find the handful of things actually worth knowing.

and the worst part is i rarely found them. id put the phone down feeling more scattered and slightly anxious, not informed. just a pile of half stories, outrage and clips. classic doomscroll.
what actually broke the habit wasnt willpower or a screen time limit. it was changing what i reach for. the way i see it theres really only two ways out. you build your own thing (a couple technical friends of mine run their own news agents, hermes setups, openclaw instances that scrape and summarise for them every morning) or you use something already built. im nowhere near technical enough to maintain my own so i just use curiouscats.ai these days. it ends instead of feeding me forever, which turned out to be the only thing that actually mattered.

curious what broke the morning scroll for other people. willpower never worked for me, swapping the app did
comment: I had the same morning loop with Twitter/IG before anything else. Swapping apps helped a bit, but I noticed even curated digests can turn into the same passive scroll if they land in the same place at the same time. What finally stuck was moving news to a fixed later slot and keeping the phone out of the bedroom entirely.
